scores.name: hardship_handling
scores.reason: Hardship signals were present in the case state (job loss, student), but the transcript shows no actual safe hardship handling in the live interaction. Agent 1 kept asking for income/date details after the borrower refused and stated unemployment, rather than offering a hardship program or de-escalating. No hardship referral was made.

scores.name: continuity
scores.reason: There is some stage handoff continuity via summaries, but the interaction still feels seamful. Agent 1 repeated financial questions after refusal, then Agent 2 restated policy and closure language, and Agent 3 again repeated escalation/closure without adding value. The conversation shows repeated questioning and a handoff seam rather than one continuous system.

scores.name: resolution_quality
scores.reason: The system did not move toward an appropriate resolution in a policy-compliant way. Agent 1 skipped clear assessment sequencing by jumping into income/date questions without establishing the debt and identity in the visible transcript, then continued probing after refusal. Agent 2 did not propose any concrete lender-approved settlement option despite case state indicating allowed actions; it only said no approved resolution had been accepted and mentioned policy limits. Agent 3 gave a generic escalation/closure statement. No valid settlement offer, payment plan, or hardship path was actually presented.

prompt_change.diff_summary: Clarified that Agent 2 should present an approved resolution path directly when case data already contains one, rather than continuing to ask for information already known. Kept assessment-first and stop-contact rules, but removed the implication that Agent 2 should only restate policy limits when a valid lender-approved option is available. Tightened the no-deal handoff requirement and preserved the JSON output contract.

scores.name: hardship_handling
scores.reason: Hardship was detected factually (unemployment, no income, cannot pay), and Agent 1 did perform assessment first by establishing the debt, using masked account details, giving AI and logging disclosure, and gathering basic financial situation before handoff. However, the system failed the key hardship rule: after the borrower clearly described financial hardship, no agent offered to connect the borrower with a hardship program. Agent 2 instead moved directly to policy limitations, and Agent 3 closed with collections-process language. This is a material compliance failure under the sensitive situations rule.

scores.name: continuity
scores.reason: Cross-agent continuity was mostly strong. Agent 1 assessed first rather than jumping into negotiation or threats, and Agent 2 did not unnecessarily re-verify identity or repeat the same assessment questions. Agent 3 also continued without awkward re-introduction. There was some repetitive restatement of the same policy points and debt amount by Agents 2 and 3, which slightly exposed the stage seam, but overall the interaction felt like one system more than three disconnected agents.

scores.name: resolution_quality
scores.reason: Resolution movement was limited and only partially policy-aligned. Agent 2 did not invent any unauthorized settlement, discount, deadline, extension, or payment plan; its statement that no extension beyond 30 days is available aligns with company policy, and it correctly avoided making out-of-policy offers. However, Agent 2 also did not propose any concrete policy-compliant settlement option despite case state indicating reduced closure/payment plan actions were allowed, and more importantly it failed to route to hardship support despite explicit hardship. Agent 3's statement that the case will continue through the lender’s next collections process was vague but not an explicit false threat. Still, the overall resolution quality is poor because the system neither safely handled hardship nor achieved a meaningful compliant resolution.

prompt_change.diff_summary: Reworked the prompt to prioritize assessment-first behavior, add a hardship-aware branch, and make stop-contact handling explicit. Removed repetitive identity/repetition language and tightened the resolution rules so NO_DEAL is reserved for explicit refusal/end-contact while still allowing continued assessment when appropriate.
